contrastMetadata.transferBoundary: Use a frequency structure when arbitrary-order counts or early equality lookup are primary; use sorting when equal-run adjacency, ordered groups, or deterministic key order is part of the result.

items.prompt: A stream must report the first position at which any value repeats. Which strategy best matches the contract?

options.text: Maintain a seen set and stop at the first repeated arrival.
Read all values, sort them, and then infer the first repeat position.
Compare only adjacent arrivals.
Build final groups before checking for a repeat.

feedback.reason: The result depends on online equality lookup and original arrival position.
feedback.details: A seen set supports both without requiring complete input or reordering.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_end: Sorting destroys arrival position unless metadata is retained and cannot stop at the first repeated arrival.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.adjacent_stream: The repeated value may be separated.

items.prompt: A report must emit every distinct key in ascending order with its count. Which direct strategy is strongest?

options.text: Sort values and scan equal runs.
Use a hash map and assume iteration is ascending.
Stop after finding one duplicate.
Compare every pair.

feedback.reason: Sorting simultaneously supplies deterministic key order and duplicate adjacency.
feedback.details: Hash counting remains viable only if the later key-ordering cost is included.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_only: A plain hash map does not guarantee ascending key order.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.pair_compare: Pairwise comparison does not create ordered groups efficiently.

items.prompt: The caller needs counts by key but explicitly accepts arbitrary group order. Which strategy avoids unused ordering work?

options.text: Use a hash frequency map.
Sort because duplicate counts are impossible without adjacency.
Use stable sorting to preserve order that the caller does not require.
Count each key by scanning the entire input repeatedly.

feedback.reason: The state is a key-to-count mapping with no ordered-output requirement.
feedback.details: Hashing directly serves that contract in expected linear time.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_required: A map updates counts wherever occurrences appear.

items.prompt: Why does sorting simplify grouping equal values?

options.text: All equal keys become one contiguous run that can be consumed with a linear scan.
Sorting automatically deletes duplicates.
Sorting implicitly creates a hash function.
Run lengths require no scan.

feedback.reason: Order turns a global equality relation into local adjacency.
feedback.details: This reusable structure is the sorting-side mechanism for duplicate grouping.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.counts_free: The runs still need to be traversed and counted.

items.prompt: Keys are comparable but intentionally not hashable. The task is to group duplicates. Which strategy remains legal?

options.text: Sort by the comparator and scan equal runs.
Use object identity as an unrelated hash and assume semantic equality.
Duplicate grouping is impossible.
Hash input indices instead of keys.

feedback.reason: Sorting needs a compatible total order, not a hash function.
feedback.details: The legal key operations can decide the strategy before asymptotic comparison.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_anyway: The hash/equality contract would be incorrect.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.no_solution: Comparison equality after sorting is sufficient.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.index_hash: Distinct indices do not group equal key values.

items.prompt: Keys are hashable but have no domain-approved total order. The result needs counts in arbitrary order. Which strategy is legal and direct?

options.text: Use a hash map keyed by semantic equality.
Sort by an incidental memory address.
Convert keys to strings without proving that string equality matches key equality.
Assume equal values are adjacent in the input.

feedback.reason: Hashing matches the available equality contract.
feedback.details: Sorting should not be forced by inventing an order unrelated to the domain.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.invent_order: An incidental order is not a domain comparator and may be unstable.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.stringify_sort: This can merge or split semantic keys incorrectly.

items.prompt: A hash frequency map is used, but the output must be deterministic across runs and environments. What additional decision is required?

options.text: Define and implement an explicit output order, such as sorting keys or preserving first-seen order.
Assume every hash table has the same iteration order.
Remove counts to make order deterministic.
Rehash repeatedly until an ascending order appears.

feedback.reason: Determinism is an observable output property.
feedback.details: A hash map can still be used, but the ordering mechanism and its cost must be explicit.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.assume_hash_order: Iteration guarantees vary and may not be value-sorted.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.drop_counts: The required data would be lost.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.rehash: Hash placement is not a sorting contract.

items.prompt: Memory is constrained, input mutation is legal, and all duplicate groups must be found. Which tradeoff can favor sorting?

options.text: An in-place sort plus run scan can avoid O(k) hash entries.
A general frequency map always uses O(1) memory.
Return only one duplicate to save memory.
Use O(n²) pair checks because memory is the only concern.

feedback.reason: Sorting can exchange more comparison work and mutation for lower auxiliary storage.
feedback.details: The chosen sorting algorithm's own memory must still be included.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_constant: It may store one entry per distinct key.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.pairwise: Time feasibility must also be considered.

items.prompt: The task needs the first occurrence index and total count for every key, emitted by first-seen order. Which design is most natural?

options.text: Scan once, storing first index and count per key, then emit according to first-seen metadata.
Sort values without positions and infer first occurrence.
Use each sorted-run start as the original first occurrence.
Use a hash map but do not store first indices or order metadata.

feedback.reason: The grouping state is equality-keyed, but the output also needs original-order metadata.
feedback.details: A map can retain both count and first position while the initial scan establishes first-seen order.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_values_only: Sorting alone loses original index and first-seen order.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.run_index: Sorted positions are not original positions.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.arbitrary_hash: The required output cannot be reconstructed reliably.

items.prompt: Different input positions contain equal values. What must be clarified before deduplicating outputs?

options.text: Whether results distinguish indexed occurrences or only distinct values.
Only ascending versus descending order.
Only initial bucket count.
How many loops the implementation uses.

feedback.reason: Duplicate handling depends on what equality means for the output.
feedback.details: A value-distinct group, an occurrence-distinct result, and a first-occurrence result require different retained information.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_direction: Order does not define occurrence identity.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_size: Representation tuning cannot replace the semantic identity contract.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.loop_count: Loop structure is not the result definition.

items.prompt: Which contracts favor a hash-based duplicate strategy? Select all that apply.

options.text: Report the first repeated arrival online.
Count every key with arbitrary output order.
Retain first indices and counts without reordering input.
Emit all groups in ascending key order with no later ordering step.

feedback.reason: Hashing is strongest for equality-keyed state, online updates, and original-position metadata.
feedback.details: Ascending groups add an ordering signal.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.arbitrary_count_order: A frequency map directly represents the result.

items.prompt: Which contracts favor sorting and run grouping? Select all that apply.

options.text: Emit groups in key order.
Compute run lengths and neighboring-group relations.
Keys have a valid comparator but no compatible hash.
Stop at the first repeated arrival in a stream.

feedback.reason: Sorting is strongest when ordered adjacency is consumed or hashing is not legal.
feedback.details: Online arrival semantics conflict with full reordering.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.online_first_repeat: Sorting requires collecting and reordering the input.

items.prompt: Which duplicate tasks can be solved correctly by either hashing or sorting? Select all that apply.

options.text: Determine whether any duplicate exists in a reorderable finite array.
Compute all value counts when either arbitrary or ordered output can be produced.
Return the set of distinct values when output ordering is flexible.
Return the first repeated arrival before the stream ends.

feedback.reason: Many duplicate contracts leave both mechanisms correct.
feedback.details: Online arrival position is a stricter boundary that favors hash state.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.first_repeat_stream: Offline sorting cannot preserve this online stopping contract directly.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.exists_duplicate: A set or sort-and-scan both work.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.all_counts: Hash counts or sorted runs both represent multiplicity.

items.prompt: Which input shapes expose duplicate-counting and grouping defects? Select all that apply.

options.text: Empty input.
Every value distinct.
Every value equal.
Only one case where the duplicate is already adjacent.

feedback.reason: The extremes expose different time, memory, and identity behavior.
feedback.details: Separated duplicates and repeated-key order should also be included in human review.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_adjacent_duplicate: This does not test separated occurrences or ordering choices.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.empty: It should produce no groups or duplicate.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.all_distinct: It maximizes hash entries and creates singleton runs.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.all_equal: It creates one maximal run and minimal distinct-key count.

items.prompt: Which questions must be answered before choosing a duplicate/grouping strategy? Select all that apply.

options.text: Is the result one early duplicate or all groups?
Must groups follow key order, first-seen order, or arbitrary order?
Are keys legally hashable, comparable, or both?
Can we assume hashing is always faster?

feedback.reason: Multiplicity alone does not determine the strategy.
feedback.details: Completeness, order, identity, memory, mutation, and legal key operations define the boundary.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_faster: That is the false heuristic being rejected.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.all_or_first: Early stopping and complete grouping differ.

items.prompt: Arrange a hash-frequency workflow.

elements.text: Increment the current value's count and any required first-index metadata.
Emit groups using the declared arbitrary, first-seen, or explicitly sorted key order.

feedback.reason: Hashing builds equality-keyed aggregate state before output.
feedback.details: The final emission order must be part of the contract rather than inherited accidentally from the table.

items.prompt: Arrange a sort-and-run grouping workflow.

elements.text: Sort values or decorated records by the grouping key.
Start a run at the first unprocessed value.
Advance while adjacent keys remain equal and accumulate run metadata.
Emit the completed group and continue with the next run.

feedback.reason: Sorting converts global equality groups into contiguous runs.
feedback.details: The run scan is linear after ordering is established.

items.prompt: Arrange the strategy decision for a duplicate/grouping problem.

elements.text: Define value equality, occurrence identity, and required metadata.
Define early detection versus complete grouping and required output order.
Check hashability, comparability, mutation, and memory constraints.
Compare hash aggregation with sort-and-run under the complete contract.

feedback.reason: The decision begins with result semantics rather than the presence of duplicates.
feedback.details: Only then can expected time, sorting cost, and space be compared fairly.

items.prompt: Count frequencies of n values with k distinct hashable keys using a hash map. Select expected time and auxiliary space.
constraints: Output storage is excluded from auxiliary space.

feedback.reason: Each occurrence performs one expected constant-time update.
feedback.details: The table retains one aggregate entry per distinct key.

items.prompt: Group n comparable values by sorting in place with O(n log n) time and O(log n) stack, then scanning runs. Select total time and auxiliary space.

feedback.reason: The linear run scan is dominated by sorting.
feedback.details: This path trades deterministic ordering and low stated auxiliary space for input mutation and comparison cost.

contrastSets.transferBoundary: Use a frequency structure when arbitrary-order counts or early equality lookup are primary; use sorting when equal-run adjacency, ordered groups, or deterministic key order is part of the result.
